---
title: "The reference models"
subtitle: "Shared · Phase 0 · `va-abi::reference`"
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status: real, working models — four of them.** `Resistor`/`Capacitor`/`Diode`/`VSource` shipped
at bootstrap (`CLAUDE.md` §9); `Bjt` was added later (§ ladder rung 6, T4.3) as the project's
first gain-capable device. All five back both `va-core`'s own bring-up and every AD-generated
model in `va-codegen`, which is checked against them.
:::

## Goal

Give `va-core` something real to solve from commit #1, and give every later AD-generated model
an independent oracle to be checked against — hand-derived, not generated.

## Where it fits

These live at the leaf of `CLAUDE.md` §2's diagram, alongside `va-ir`:

```
                         [va-abi]
                    ModelInstance/StampSink
                    + hand-written reference/
                              │
                    ┌─────────┴─────────┐
                    │                   │
              [va-codegen]         [va-core]
           (checked against       (solves them
            these models)          directly too)
```

`va-codegen`'s generated resistor/diode reproduce these exact stamps — that equivalence is
itself one of T2's own tests (`t2-codegen/02-lowering.qmd`).

## The idea

Every model here shares one job: turn its own device physics into the four `StampSink` channels
(`residual`, `jacobian`, `charge`, `dcharge`) at whatever operating point `x` currently is.
Four of the five (`Resistor`/`Capacitor`/`Diode`/`VSource`) are two-terminal and mostly share
three small helpers in `reference/mod.rs`:

```rust
fn stamp_conductance(sink, p, n, i, g)  // residual ± i, Jacobian ± g, the canonical 2×2 stamp
fn stamp_charge(sink, p, n, q, c)       // the same shape, into the charge channel
fn voltage_across(x, p, n) -> f64       // x[p] - x[n], out-of-range = 0 V (ground)
```

`Bjt` (three-terminal) is the one exception — its base/collector/emitter currents cross-couple,
so it stamps its own 3×3 block by hand rather than reusing the two-terminal helpers.

## The code

**`Resistor`** — `I = V/R`, the simplest possible stamp:

```rust
fn load(&self, x: &[f64], sink: &mut dyn StampSink) {
    let [p, n] = self.terminals;
    let v = voltage_across(x, p, n);
    let i = self.g * v;
    stamp_conductance(sink, p, n, i, self.g);   // g = 1/R, constant
}
```

**`Capacitor`** — `Q = C·V`, contributing *only* to the charge channel (an open circuit in DC;
`va-transient`'s companion model differentiates the charge channel in time):

```rust
fn load(&self, x: &[f64], sink: &mut dyn StampSink) {
    let [p, n] = self.terminals;
    let v = voltage_across(x, p, n);
    let q = self.c * v;
    stamp_charge(sink, p, n, q, self.c);
}
```

**`Diode`** — the Shockley law, `I = Is·(exp(Vd/(N·Vt)) − 1)`, with an *exact* analytic
conductance (a wrong Jacobian silently destroys Newton convergence — `CLAUDE.md` §5's AD
discipline applies here too, even though this model has no AD in it):

```rust
pub fn current(&self, vd: f64) -> f64 { self.is * ((vd / self.nvt).exp() - 1.0) }
pub fn conductance(&self, vd: f64) -> f64 { (self.is / self.nvt) * (vd / self.nvt).exp() }
```

`VT_NOMINAL` (`0.025_865` V) is the project's thermal-voltage constant at its nominal 300.15 K
simulation temperature — chosen to match QSPICE's own default `TNOM` exactly (§ the T6.3
tutorial's "temperature convention" fix), not an arbitrary round 300 K.

**`VSource`** — the one model here that introduces an *extra* unknown, its own branch current,
rather than just wiring two terminals:

```rust
fn load(&self, x: &[f64], sink: &mut dyn StampSink) {
    let [p, n, b] = self.terminals;
    // Constraint row: V(p) - V(n) - value = 0 (not a KCL sum!).
    sink.residual(b, x[p] - x[n] - self.value);
    sink.jacobian(b, p, 1.0);
    sink.jacobian(b, n, -1.0);
    // The branch current flows out of p, into n.
    sink.residual(p, x[b]);
    sink.residual(n, -x[b]);
    sink.jacobian(p, b, 1.0);
    sink.jacobian(n, b, -1.0);
}
```

Its `branch` unknown overrides `unknown_kind` to report `UnknownKind::Branch` — the first real
use of that Interface β extension (`shared/01-interfaces.qmd`): `gmin`-stepping must never shunt
a voltage source's own constraint row.

**`Bjt`** — a simplified (textbook) Ebers-Moll NPN, `Ib = Ibe + Ibc`, `Ic = Icc − Ibc`, where
`Icc = Is·(exp(Vbe/Vt) − exp(Vbc/Vt))`. The reverse (`Vbc`-driven) term is what keeps this model
physically sane into saturation — unlike a naive `Ic = βF·Ib`, `Ic` here self-limits as `V(c)`
drops toward `V(b)`, the real Ebers-Moll saturation behavior, with no separate clamp needed.
Its Jacobian has a structural identity worth knowing: every column sums to exactly zero
(`Ib + Ic + Ie_row = 0` for any bias, since KCL at the three terminals must balance) — a cheap,
powerful sanity check this model's own tests assert directly, independent of the FD check.

## It works

Every model has a finite-difference test asserting its analytic Jacobian matches a central
difference (`CLAUDE.md` §5, non-negotiable):

```console
$ cargo test -p va-abi
test reference::diode::tests::conductance_matches_finite_difference ... ok
test reference::bjt::tests::partials_match_finite_difference ... ok
test reference::bjt::tests::jacobian_columns_sum_to_zero ... ok
test reference::bjt::tests::forward_active_gives_current_gain_near_beta_f ... ok
test reference::vsource::tests::only_the_branch_current_is_a_constraint_row ... ok
...
```

`Bjt` additionally checks `Ic/Ib ≈ βF` deep in forward-active bias (well away from saturation,
where the reverse term is negligible) — a real physical-gain check, not just a Jacobian shape
check.

`tests/pipeline.rs` (workspace-level) hand-stamps a two-resistor divider plus a capacitor into
one `DenseStamp` and checks the assembled Jacobian/charge match the textbook answer by hand —
the same divider `va-core`'s own bring-up test solves through Newton.

## Limitations

Every model here is deliberately minimal, stated in its own doc comment, not hidden:
`Resistor`/`Capacitor` have no temperature coefficient or voltage dependence; `Diode` has no
junction/diffusion capacitance, series resistance, or breakdown; `Bjt` has no Early effect, no
ohmic parasitics, and — found empirically while tuning the ring-oscillator demo (§ T4.3) — no
saturation-charge smoothing, so a large-amplitude transient can eventually push it into a regime
where the embedded-pair LTE estimator stops agreeing at any step size. None of these are bugs;
they're the textbook-simplest model that's still analytically exact within its own stated scope.

## What's next

`t1-frontend/01-lexing.qmd` — the language front end that will eventually let a *compiled*
`.va` model (via `va-codegen`) stand in for any of these, starting from source text instead of a
hand-written Rust struct.
